# 📝 Notes

Apr 15, 2026

## 8am AI\!

Invited [David Olsson](mailto:david@atomic47.co) [eric@scroll.ca](mailto:eric@scroll.ca) [giroday@gmail.com](mailto:giroday@gmail.com) [hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com](mailto:hanif.janmohamed@gmail.com) [Fulvio Ciano](mailto:fulvio@atomic47.co) [scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com](mailto:scott@driftresourcetechnologies.com) [travis@atomic47.co](mailto:travis@atomic47.co) [Jason Kryski](mailto:jason@mttr.ca) [Jen Boger](mailto:jen@atomic47.co) [Shabraj Shahid](mailto:shabraj@mttr.ca) [spencer@ratiomachina.com](mailto:spencer@ratiomachina.com) [Ashley Beckett](mailto:ashley@mttr.ca) [graham@ratiomachina.com](mailto:graham@ratiomachina.com) [Bailey Klinger](mailto:bwklinger@gmail.com) ~~[andre.bertram3@gmail.com](mailto:andre.bertram3@gmail.com)~~

Attachments [8am AI\!](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/event?eid=aW52N2w4MWJvNjJqYm5zYmQxZTMzYjllNmZfMjAyNjA0MTVUMTUwMDAwWiBkYXZpZEBhdG9taWM0Ny5jbw)

Meeting records [Transcript](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1jV7_RqpKuimgM9nRD8cdzIxVgkaK_2Iuv0xUFNTAi2Q/edit?usp=drive_web&tab=t.4rg03nt8c1j3) 

### Summary

Team members evaluated AI development workflows and future skill requirements with shifting educational and professional demands.

**Claude ecosystem development workflow**  
Participants explored leveraging high token capacity models for complex code generation and orchestration. The group emphasized using Claude for full-stack application development and repository analysis.

**Changing workforce and education**  
The team debated how AI reliance affects developer discipline and junior hiring needs. They concluded that soft skills and humanities are becoming more valuable than technical tasks.

**Human feedback and grounding**  
The primary decision was to implement owner-executor workflows to prevent AI hallucinations via human critique. This grounding method ensures outputs remain tethered to real-world context.

*Rate this Summary:* [Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=true) or [Not Helpful](https://google.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_4YkxrBAaiTVqYCi?isGoogler=false&isHelpful=false)

### Decisions

We've **updated the Decisions section** using your feedback.

Let us know what you think: [Helpful](https://bit.ly/4j0NRI9) or [Not Helpful](https://bit.ly/4p4GaCr)

## ALIGNED

* **Strategy for engaging small businesses** The strategy for engaging small businesses is set to prioritize offering standardized, vertical-specific solutions over conducting open-ended discovery processes to effectively lower the barrier to entry for collaboration.

* **Owner-executor workflow model adopted** The owner-executor workflow model is adopted as a strategic framework to improve human feedback loops and mitigate algorithmic hallucinations.

### Next steps

- [ ] \[Jason Kryski\] Replace OpenClaw: Implement solution using Claudes native Telegram integration.

- [ ] \[Jason Kryski\] Create Sessions: Build required T-Mux sessions.

- [ ] \[David Olsson\] Send Transcript: Ensure Ying receives meeting transcripts to use question content in her session.

- [ ] \[The group\] Suggest Class Exercises: Provide exercise suggestions. Submit ideas for incorporation into the class.

### Details

*Did the screenshots in this section make your notes [better](https://goo.gle/44IH7J9) or [worse](https://goo.gle/4b5ZWtJ)?*

* **Demonstration of Crush Dynamics Digital Twin**: David Olsson presented a web-based 3D simulation environment using Babylon.js for Crush Dynamics, which visualizes a mathematical model for fermentation as a digital twin. The visualization is currently basic, but the underlying data is live data from the company, which David is using to learn what is required for further development ([00:00:00](#00:00:00)). The simulation includes avatars that are amusingly seen walking through walls and getting bigger and smaller because it is built in a game environment ([00:03:10](#00:03:10)).

  ![][image1]

* **AI Usage and Token Tracking**: David Olsson discussed tracking the usage of their Max account tokens, specifically mentioning the progress bars in the Claude desktop application settings under "usage," which show daily, weekly (all models), and Sonnet-only limits. This tracking is done manually, but the Max account is noted as being very generous in its token allowance, enabling extensive use of the Opus model ([00:04:49](#00:04:49)).

* **Comparison of Opus and Sonnet**: David Olsson noted a significant difference in capability between the Opus and Sonnet models, particularly in managing development. David also stated that they found Opus to be better than other leading models, specifically referencing a version of OpenAI ([00:05:41](#00:05:41)).

* **Utilizing Claude Code for Application Development**: David Olsson detailed a development process where a rigorous application specification was created using Claude's Opus model, and then the specification was brought into Claude Code inside the desktop app with a simple prompt: "Build this" ([00:05:41](#00:05:41)). Claude Code built the application phase by phase and allowed for quick refinements, such as changing the color scheme, without requiring them to spend excessive time reading code ([00:06:45](#00:06:45)).

* **Emergence of Claude's Full Application Development Stack**: David Olsson highlighted that the Claude ecosystem, including Claude Code, now offers a full application development stack comparable to environments like Replit or Vercel, enabling deployment of apps. A recent release allows the API to be used within the built artifacts, meaning applications can be developed and hosted as interactive artifacts with AI capabilities, such as a hosted chatbot ([00:07:52](#00:07:52)).

* **Increased Productivity and Workflow Quality**: David Olsson shared that their ability to use Claude has led to a significant increase in productivity and higher quality output by leveraging the high token capacity of the Max account ([00:08:44](#00:08:44)). By using Claude Co-work pointed at a directory with 100 repositories, they were able to synthesize millions of lines of code and produce 48 articulate reports across all repositories in a few days ([00:09:47](#00:09:47)).

* **Token Economics and Workflow Optimization**: The conversation touched on the high token consumption of certain non-sanctioned tools like OpenClaw, which led to account freezes ([00:10:51](#00:10:51)). David Olsson explained that they are working to stay ahead of the limits, and by paying more for Opus 4.6 and Max tokens, they can achieve a headspace where they are unfettered by token economics, allowing for better execution of parallel work via orchestration ([00:12:48](#00:12:48)) ([00:18:17](#00:18:17)).

* **Need for a Focused Claude Workflow Discussion**: Jason Kryski expressed interest in learning more about David Olsson's specific development workflow using Claude. David Olsson agreed that a separate meeting on the topic would be beneficial to break down the steps and share strategies, noting that the same strategies apply to knowledge work like analyzing code repositories from a business perspective ([00:19:28](#00:19:28)).

* **Advanced Code Auditing and Automation**: David Olsson described running expensive slash commands, which orchestrate the execution of 10 or 11 reports from various skills and templates to perform a full orchestration after code creation, which they deem necessary as even a security audit is insufficient ([00:20:33](#00:20:33)). Juan affirmed this automation trend by admitting that they now ask large language models to handle tasks like generating commits and pushes, noting that the commit messages produced are superior ([00:21:41](#00:21:41)).

* **Leveraging AI for Legacy Codebases**: Juan explained that their current work involves building scaffolding around a mature, existing codebase, and they are increasingly relying on Claude for better code production, following the structure that Claude suggests for managing repositories and documentation ([00:22:40](#00:22:40)). Juan noted a transition toward using Claude for complex tasks like transforming Jira tickets into Statements of Work and drafting architecture decisions, moving toward a preference for the Claude ecosystem ([00:23:52](#00:23:52)).

* **AI's Role in Legacy System Evolution**: Fulvio Ciano suggested that AI provides a solution for managing legacy projects by building a descriptive corpus of the existing code, running optimization phases, and evolving the system without restarting the whole project ([00:25:02](#00:25:02)). Juan confirmed this objective, aiming to use AI to clean up, integrate, and simplify the existing solution by identifying complicated gaps ([00:26:22](#00:26:22)).

* **Developer Discipline and Role Changes**: Juan expressed concern that developers' discipline is disappearing as they rely on AI-created skills and sophisticated prompts to perform tasks like checking architecture and creating branches ([00:26:22](#00:26:22)). This shift means that current developers are primarily tasked with testing and fixing the AI-generated code, effectively acting as Quality Assurance, which may lead to the "flattening" of development teams ([00:27:47](#00:27:47)).

* **Changing Needs in Technical Education**: Ying discussed the changing landscape of education, noting that students no longer need to learn prompt engineering because models like Claude or Grok can generate highly optimized prompts ([00:28:56](#00:28:56)). Ying expressed concern that if students do not build basic skills and understand the internal workings of AI tools, they will be unprepared for future challenges as they will lack the ability to evaluate the quality of the AI's output, potentially struggling to secure entry-level jobs ([00:30:27](#00:30:27)).

* **Shifting Skill Requirements for Junior Hires**: David Olsson shared an anecdote about an entrepreneur who, despite using AI, hired a junior programmer to build simple applications that could not be achieved solely with skills and chatbots, highlighting that specific software development needs remain ([00:31:59](#00:31:59)). Juan and Bailey Klinger agreed that future junior hires should possess strong soft skills like design, taste, communication, empathy, and the ability to translate business needs into technical requirements, filling the weaknesses of current AI tools ([00:39:03](#00:39:03)).

* **The Value of Humanities in the AI Era**: The participants noted a complete shift in value from traditional science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) skills to human-centric disciplines like arts, history, design, communication, and empathy, which are difficult for AI to replicate ([00:41:13](#00:41:13)). David Olsson suggested that this marks a return to valuing the humanities, which had waned following the rise of STEM, and that one's "why" (values, principles) is a significant aspect that people bring to the table ([00:42:49](#00:42:49)).

* **Need for Practical, Real-World Experience in Education**: Ying sought advice on how to make their AI course more valuable than just teaching prompt engineering, particularly how to prepare students for the changing job market ([00:47:20](#00:47:20)). David Olsson suggested that practical, high-fidelity simulations and real-world experiences are crucial, and that they should focus on how to thrive in the new environment ([00:48:37](#00:48:37)).

* **Strategies for Student Engagement with Businesses**: Bailey Klinger suggested that students focus on engaging with small and medium-sized businesses that are low-tech and early in the AI transition, helping them integrate AI into their operations ([00:51:01](#00:51:01)). David Olsson and Bailey Klinger advised that students offer specific, full solutions for standardized problems in certain business verticals (e.g., voice agents for restaurants) rather than engaging in time-consuming discovery processes ([00:54:24](#00:54:24)) ([00:57:48](#00:57:48)).

* **Maintaining Relevance in an AI-Enhanced Workforce**: Juan asserted that the professional world will continue to require people who can make decisions, generate ideas, and maintain curiosity in problem-solving. They emphasized the need for wisdom and a deep understanding of how to learn and self-actualize, suggesting that individuals with these capacities, even those using AI for every task, will remain employed. This involves focusing more on the context of a system rather than just the immediate solution ([01:00:39](#01:00:39)).

* **Incorporating Curiosity into Learning Activities**: Following Juan's departure, Ying requested suggestions for class exercises, leading David Olsson to elaborate on the importance of encouraging students to use their curiosity significantly. The goal is for students to utilize tools to uncover creative capacities within themselves, a process David Olsson related to their own experiences in a standardized coding environment. This creativity is accessible in all spaces and often emerges through conversation ([01:01:53](#01:01:53)).

* **Exploring Peer Groups and Paired Work Concepts**: David Olsson shared two current concepts they are developing: the use of peer groups for side-by-side learning and paired programming ([01:01:53](#01:01:53)). Paired programming, a concept introduced long ago involving two people working on the same problem simultaneously, evolved into the idea of "paired work" or "co-pilot" in current contexts. David Olsson suggested borrowing the concept of an "owner and the executor" from software development for paired work, where the owner provides human critique to prevent the executor from getting lost in the work ([01:03:08](#01:03:08)).

* **Implementing the Owner-Executor Feedback Loop for Grounding**: The "owner-executor" system provides a crucial feedback loop that prevents "hallucination" by introducing an external human perspective to the work ([01:03:08](#01:03:08)). This mechanism, which David Olsson refers to as "grounding," helps break the tight loop that occurs when an individual is essentially talking to themself with the same Large Language Model (LLM). Ying agreed with this concept, noting that they frequently experience these endless loops when entering questions and receiving executor prompts from AI ([01:04:05](#01:04:05)).

* **Recognizing the Value of Transcripts and Learning Loops**: David Olsson highlighted the transcript as a highly powerful tool, recognizing its value for documenting intelligence and leadership as discussions evolve from scripted to unscripted. They concluded that the learning loop has improved significantly within the models over the past few years, indicating that understanding the learning process is currently the most essential and precarious position for individuals and small companies ([01:04:05](#01:04:05)). David Olsson had to conclude the discussion to attend to another meeting and to eat ([01:05:06](#01:05:06)).

* **The Importance of Human Feedback and Contextual Grounding**: Ying expressed a preference for human feedback over even beautiful output from ChatGPT because AI lacks essential contextual knowledge, such as the specific dynamics of the Okonagen region and its small businesses ([01:05:06](#01:05:06)). David Olsson reinforced the idea that any grounding—such as through surveys or market research—provides a broad base and prevents the user from "hallucinating" by validating information with external data not derived from an LLM. They noted that a human-in-the-loop validation process is critical ([01:06:03](#01:06:03)).

* **Leveraging Market Research and Averaging for Insights**: David Olsson suggested that even with a low 10% survey response rate, market research is a valuable tool because it costs nothing but time ([01:06:03](#01:06:03)). They explained that insights are essentially averaged information, and by continuing to average different data points, a person can reduce the inherent concerns of averaging, similar to how an LLM operates. David Olsson stressed that market research allows for multiple data points to be used to create a sense of things ([01:06:51](#01:06:51)).

*You should review Gemini's notes to make sure they're accurate. [Get tips and learn how Gemini takes notes](https://support.google.com/meet/answer/14754931)*
